quotes.verbatim_quote:
A backdoor dominant is a dominant 7" chord that substitutes the V7
chord for a bVII7 chord approaching the | chord by a whole step. This
works because the bVII7 has a lot of notes in common with an altered
V7 chord.

Example: Bb7-Cmaj/
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the backdoor dominant concept with a concrete example, a harmonic device that recurs throughout the chapter's analysis of Stella.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A hybrid ii-V-I is when a minor ii-V-i is mixed with a major ii-V-l. The ii
and V are borrowed from the minor key, but they resolve to the major |.

Example: Dmin7(b5)-G7(b9)-Cmaj7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the hybrid ii-V-I as a distinct harmonic construction, giving the reader a transferable concept applicable across many other jazz standards.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This compositional technique
is used to add a sense of surprise. When you hear the minor ii-V being
played, it gives you the sense that it will resolve to a minor chord, but
then unexpectedly, it resolves to a major chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains the perceptual/expressive function of the hybrid ii-V-I, giving the reader an aural target when listening or improvising.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The chord changes that jazz musicians play are different.
They have the same essence, but because of the influence of mu-
sicians like Miles Davis and the advent of the Real Book, the chord
changes have evolved into more traditional jazz harmony. If you were
to learn the chord changes from the original version, you wouldn't be
playing the same stuff as other jazz musicians will on gigs and jam
sessions.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes a practical performance rule: use the evolved Real Book changes, not the original film version, to be compatible with other jazz musicians.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Blue = Bb major (parent key)

Green = D minor (iii chord of parent)
Orange = Eb major (IV chord of parent)
‘Purple: = F major (V major or parent)
‘Yellow = G major (VI7b13 of parent)
Red = C minor (ii of parent)
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the analytical framework used throughout the chapter, mapping all Roman numerals to the parent key while color-coding secondary tonal centers.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The first thing to note is that we have a series of unresolved ii-V chord
progressions within the first 8 bars. We have seen these before. This
just means the progressions do not resolve to the | chord but move
somewhere else. The key center is being eluded to, yet never comes
home.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the defining harmonic characteristic of Stella's opening — tonally evasive unresolved ii-Vs — which governs how the entire song should be understood and navigated.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The ii chord is being added in front of the backdoor dominant. There-
fore, the Bbmin7-Eb7 is really acting as a backdoor ii-V into the Fmaj7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the backdoor dominant concept to the full backdoor ii-V, showing how a preceding ii chord can be incorporated into the substitution pattern.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When we come across songs like Stella, it can be beneficial to take a
step back and map out the notes we have available to us. While scales
are not musical by themselves, they can help us conceptualize which
notes we can choose from. From there, we can start to build melodies
and make sense of how to connect the harmony in our lines.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the note-mapping approach as a conceptual scaffolding tool for improvisation over harmonically dense material.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
One exercise | like to do to keep things simple and help me start to
improvise is create quarter note solos. This means | stick to a quarter
note rhythm in all of my note choices. | take out the rhythmic compo-
nent from my improvisation and just focus on navigating the changes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes a specific practice technique that isolates harmonic navigation from rhythm, reducing cognitive load when learning to improvise over complex changes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Bar 25 marks the last section of the song. The first two bars are the
same as the beginning of the tune, but this time we continue on with a
cycle of 4ths. This cycle of 4ths is different than we have seen before
in that they are a series of minor ii-V’s.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Distinguishes the closing cycle-of-4ths progression as built from minor ii-Vs rather than dominant ii-Vs, a structural distinction that affects scale choice during improvisation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
But there are two ways to perceive the Ab7 in bar 8. You can think of
it as a V7 of the IV chord (fairly common), or you can recognize it as
a backdoor dominant moving into the | chord in bar 10.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Models dual harmonic interpretation of a single chord, teaching the reader to hold multiple functional readings simultaneously rather than locking into one.
